translated: When did you celebrate them? On April twenty-sixth. Uh-huh, yes, and this year, God willing, we are going to celebrate it as if it were the fiftieth anniversary, the golden wedding anniversary, right? *laughs* Going to Benidorm is my passion, it's something I like, but you also get tired when you go so many times. Uh-huh, you already know everything there, so why go there? A place I haven't seen, I mean, well, what I want to say is yes, but in winter, the only place with an atmosphere is in Benidorm, you know that. But everyone goes there, and you can't walk through the streets, it's all full of people. Uh-huh, yes, in winter you can't go anywhere, that's what happens around here, around the Mediterranean, there's always a lot of people. *laughs* *snaps tongue* *laughs* *nods* Then there comes a time when, after four, five, or six years or more, in summer we started closing in the afternoons, and then we had more free time. But until then, I worked for a little while. I was a hairdresser, but when I married him, I learned the trade, and the dogs are getting on my nerves. *laughs* Both of them, it's okay. Yes, it is a problem because I'm listening for the phone, thinking it's going to ring. They're so annoying, for God's sake. *laughs* The neighbors call to complain. We had a very good teacher, the best teacher around here in Valencia, and then he took us, and we went to Pollos. *looks at* We started in Foyos, I think, no, Meliana, or in Meliana, and he charged, of course, he charged each of us, so we gave him some money. You know what happens, and then he lived precisely around there, near Pérez Galdós, almost in front of where we had the butcher shop, and as I said, we went to a lot of places, we went on excursions, we danced everywhere. He was a guy for NGOs, we went to Jaén. What a trip he wants us to take in September, which we do every year. We don't go every year; we have a house that was my father's, then we tore it down, so we are, hmm, we were, well, now it's just me, two girls, three, three, and me, four, and we tore down the house that was my father's, and the... We made everything new, and there is a beautiful house there in Jaén. It says yes *trn laughs* that you have some memory lapses now that your memory is *haedno trn laughs*. During the year, people work here and there and don't go to the village, but when the festivals come, since they practically only have one festival a year, uhm, when the festivals arrive, well, no one works anymore. They close everything, and everyone goes to the festivals. No one works, and everyone dedicates themselves solely to the festivities. In the morning, they have a procession. When the procession ends, they go to the square, eeh, the music plays, and everyone dances. But in the morning, they do it in the morning. They tie it to the four corners, as they call it, yes, but they only have a procession one day. The other days, what do they do in the morning? I stay at home. Well, then they tie the bulls at the entrance of the bulls. Ah, it horrifies me. I stay at home, and he goes, and there is also an esplanade where you can see at least more than four, five, or six kilometers.

Text_participant: En un lugar de la mancha de cuyo nombre lo quiere acordarme . 1020_5344 No ha mucho tiempo que vivía un hidalgo del orden . 6065_10030 Lanza en artillero a dar antigua rocín fla hidalgo corredor una olla de algo más que baja que carnero salpicón las más noches duelos y quebrantos los sábados retejas los viernes un palomino de añadidura a los domingos consumían las tres partes de su hacienda . 10320_19489
translated: In a place in La Mancha, whose name I do not wish to remember, not long ago lived a nobleman of the rank of a gentleman, with a lance in the rack, an old shield, a lean horse, and a greyhound for racing. A pot of something more than beef stew, hash most nights, eggs and bacon on Saturdays, lentils on Fridays, and a pigeon as an extra on Sundays consumed three-quarters of his income.

translated:
Sure, here's the translation of the provided Spanish text to English:

"Well, and tomorrow morning we have to come here to the doctor, after the doctor what do we do, do we go to the countryside or what? No, that's you who sets the pace, but we'll have to take out the animals we have at home. You see, we have four dogs there, and I understand that I also take walks because the dogs are there at home. So, you have the dogs so you can take walks, sir? No, because it's normal, he does it for me because I take walks and he takes walks too, haha, and that's it. It must be a big house, right, to have four dogs? No, it's just that the dogs, some out of pity, others because we raised them, and well, my son can tell you, not me. I was working, of course, that's why I say, but who brought you because I was at work? My grandson picked me up and brought me here. And who else, uh, well, who else? My grandson brought me here, and who else was in the car? Uh, uh, oh Paloma, yes, Paloma, my daughter-in-law. Oh, the poor thing also has her work, taking care of grandpa, and with the ointments, and this and that, that's why I say she has her work too. And look, and we tell it, as they say. Well, and in the afternoon, do we have to take a load of firewood to the restaurant or what? What do you mean, do we take a load of firewood to the restaurant in the afternoon? Oh, well, that's your business. Yes, but you'll have to help me load it. Of course, I'll help you whenever I can, I help with what I can, but since it's so little what I can do with this thing with my hand, this is from the accident in Picassent or Picassent at the cabin, uh-huh. And what is there to take care of there, what do we have there to take care of, plants? Well, moving arms, moving this, moving that, and I, since I have the misfortune that the train got me, I can't do what everyone else does there, and of course, I have a good time there. And music, but let's see, here it says they take a nap, so they all go to sleep or what? Where, here, uh, in the..." At school, we each lie down however we want on those chairs that are put there for everyone, and there's a lot of noise with people dragging them around, of course, to get a spot. That way, they don't lose their keys. This is pointless because I keep mine here, and since I don't have any bad habits, haha, I just go on like that.

translated: In a place in La Mancha, whose name I do not wish to remember, not long ago lived a nobleman, one of those with a lance in the rack, an old shield, a skinny horse, and a greyhound for racing. A pot of something more beef than mutton, hash most nights, duels and losses on Saturdays, lentils on Fridays, and some pigeon as an extra on Sundays consumed three-quarters of his estate.

translated: Well, in guiñote, they double the card, uh-huh, and you can call twenty depending on if it's the king, looks at trn, it's not the king or the jack and the horse, I don't know, something like that, maybe they call twenty, uh-huh. And in brisca, what do you play with? We call it the malilla, the ace, and then the malilla, which is like the second most valuable, worth ten points, the king, hmm. No, well, we play with the horse, the two, or in another town, the three, you know. So, the ace is worth eleven points, the other ten, the horse, no, the king four, the horse three, of course, the king is the most valuable, I say. Well, then we play brisca differently, laughs trn laughs. When they think it's ready, they put it on for us, and then we comment on things, uh-huh. This is from May, yes, of course, and I suppose the day center won't be open in August either. In August, no, in August, no. What are they going to do in August? What are we going to do, uhg? If you ask my husband, he'll tell you right away because he goes to the village, which is in Teruel, the village. Ah, Manzanera, I don't know if you've heard of it, Manzanera, yes. Well, this is a hamlet, but I imagine we'll go up. So, do you go with your daughter, or do you go alone to your house there? Eh, at least we'll go up because I got my license and then left it at that. Ah, yes, I mean, she's the one who drives, of course. Yes, she got it years ago, I got it, yes. And what car did you have, maybe a six hundred? No, a six hundred that broke down, oh, it was an old car too. Ah, so you bought it from, oh, what a mess, who am I? You are María Teresa, you're my daughter. Ah, okay, that's why trn laughs. We could do that, of course, we could gather all the siblings in one place, right? Because everyone goes their own way, even though they're already retired and all, you know. But no, the one I mentioned, I said my brother, Uncle Jo, my son trn, I'm going to get my license just like that, oh, because you say, wow, I don't have to do this, well, yes, if everyone else does it, well, that's it, laughs trn. Well, nothing, well, look, at Pepsi Cola, there was a second cousin of mine, we were family, a... There, when there was more work in the summer, he had the opportunity because otherwise he would have worked in construction, meaning he has never really been unemployed. He also helped people in the fields there, of course, because in Castellar there are people who work in agriculture and such, and if they needed him, he would go too. And then, when he retired, and I had to go to the center where the bus passes by, we moved here, and we already had the house here, of course.

Your sister has some, so we bought five sacks this year. Five, well next year six. Hmm, yes, I live, but as I understand it, they haven't worked on it, but I know, and I know how to work. Man, I don't fail with flowers. Show me the flowers you want, none will fail you. No, because you have to know how to arrange them, how, and yes, yes, yes, the flowers from there. I told him, buy me some new flowers. *laughs* Because I know what I have. I don't have a penny. Oh, I go for the newspaper, and he gives me three euros. They cost two fifty, and I pocket the fifty cents and forget about it, and two or three days later, I'm missing fifty cents. *laughs* And I carry fifty. Goodbye, Don Julio Pastor, who is a notary, has three sons and a daughter who are notaries. When he was leaving, he had an apartment in Madrid, another in Benicassim, and no, he couldn't. I said, but Don Julio, what should I do? I said, sell the one in Madrid, the one in Mallorca, or I don't know, keep the one in Benicassim, but both, when you go to Madrid, you're paying for two weeks. Well, you're right, I said, of course, you have the one by the beach, don't sell that one, but the one in Madrid, yes, why? And sometimes they don't pay me, and I say, well, better sell it, and when you go to Madrid, what do you spend? A hundred, two hundred, and back home without more expenses. That's what he did. Well, this gentleman was going to Benicassim and gave me a key and said, this key, not even to my wife. *laughs* And enter the office, and he had, ugh, I couldn't open the drawer of bundles, one or more taken from thousands of pesetas, thousands, thousands, thousands. I would close it, lock it, and I had the keys on a little table at my house. And the son comes, the third one, Alfonso, the others were married, and the young one was very young, and he came with a convertible car at this hour of v... It was around the month of July, three or four young women, Conrado, my father's keys, I give you your father's keys, not the office ones, I say I don't have them, yes you do, I say I don't, I don't have them, oh she got mad, you'll remember this, well, I don't have them, they were at my house, but his father told me, not even to my wife, and now that we've done some things at the notary, deeds and stuff, I say Fernando Alfonso, I'm waiting, shut up, damn it, everyone should be like you, I say now, now laughs trn honest when he wanted the money, no, he wanted the office to take it and off he went, now, now I'm good, oh, they didn't want me to retire, I retired at sixty-two, they didn't want me to, yes, I've been diligent and honest, and the doctor who operated on my daughter twice, or once, two C-sections, two C-sections, Dr. Daniel Fernández, a great doctor, how many grandchildren do you have, huh, how many children do I have, how many grandchildren do you have, two, two, both just like their mother, these women can't give birth laughs trn well for that trn laughs trn laughs trn laughs three or four days at the Monasterio de Piedra, to the Palafox cinema, they were showing some movie, and home, the year of the Valencia flood, uh-huh, fifty-seven, and we went with two hundred pesetas and came back with one hundred fifty laughs trn laughs if the soles broke here, let's see who walked, it caught your foot, and friends came, I say I don't want to go out today, where was I going with that laughs trn oh until I earned nine pesetas playing pelota, I lived with someone from the money, I gave him five, five, we went to twenty, I gave him five or six points, we started playing, and I knew I would win, or I gave him ten when we started, he already had ten, and I won, and with that money, I lived laughs trn I played pelota very well, and he wanted to beat me, and I lived off him, but what game, what game is that, fronton, fronton, ah Basque pelota, Basque pelota, because in Valencia, I came to play, we came to play in Valencia with the one from Museros there in Pelayo uh-huh they gave us a... We didn't see the little ball like that, laughs, no, no, really, they were great, and then we told them to come play in Teruel, and there, they messed up too, they couldn't handle that ball, laughs. Of course, we played with a ball that weighed two hundred and fifty grams, a ball like that, and they were like a flash, and we couldn't catch the ball, you know, and they were used to the ball and stairs because the frontón in Aragón, in Aragón it's a frontón, well, two walls and a frontón, there are no stairs, and they don't throw the ball here. I wasn't good with my left hand, but my right hand, yes, I always played on the right, they looked for me on the left, my brother was very good, excellent, well, I was good on the right. But anyway, we went down to Teruel, my mother and I, I was about sixteen years old or so, wearing some little espadrilles, and it was a day, let's say it was twenty-something degrees below zero, freezing, to buy a headstall for a donkey or something, I went with my poor mother, with the Virgin, because I call her the Virgin, and we went in and started, we went in to see the headstall, and I saw it there because of the glass, oh Lord, we made a mistake, let's go to that store next door, she says, no ma'am, she didn't turn around and says, look where they are, oh my mother, the headstalls were here, okay, and it was a mirror, and the mirror showed that the headstalls were there, oh, we made a mistake, sir, let's go to that store next door, she says, no, no, and she had never been out, she didn't know what a mirror was. Well, in the pharmacy, there are some formulas to make, but apart from that, it's just that someone sits in front of the computer and doesn't stick a single label, and there are a ton of prescriptions to sort out and stamp and prepare because you have to prepare them according to whether they are for pensioners, private insurance, or whatever, each thing goes to a specific place for the prescriptions, and if you put one where it doesn't belong, it's no good, and they mark it as void. translated:
We talk about your brother Pepe. Pepe, who is married, is married to Cochín and lives in Benimaclet next to, what's it called, the thing with the quereguilles. No, not the quereguilles, no, no, the quereguilles warehouse. The warehouse, you know, where Pachá used to be. Well, um, laughs, you know, where Pachá was, yes, the nightclub that was a nightclub later. No, no, Uncle Pepe lives there. Uncle Pepe lived next to the warehouse, okay, nods, okay. And what can you tell me? And Aunt Conchín, what about her? Aunt Conchín, well, she's, well, a bit under the weather, but laughs, like you, is she worse or better than you? How is she? laughs, laughs, laughs, playing there, playing a lot. That's good. Where did your husband work? He worked at Lladró. And what happened to your father? Tell José Luis because he doesn't know. Well, he took the car, the car that dad used, the motorcycle, okay, and he took the car and it hit him on the motorcycle. And how many years ago was that? Many years, laughs, he doesn't talk anymore. And the kids were all small, and she took care of all of us. And now it's our turn to take care of her. She's a courageous mother. We didn't have this house; it was a house on a road that went to the cemetery. No, it's a bit confusing there. You got it mixed up. Yes, we lived in Pellis, we lived there when dad was around, and then when dad left, they came to live here, right? And living there in the neighborhood that was called, and here, no, she didn't live. No, that's it, mom, from there to here, the cemetery thing is from when you were young, when you were young and lived next to your mother. Ah, there in Benimaclet, uh-hum, nods, yes, then they bought this house later. Yes, I bought it to get married, laughs, laughs, that's it, this topic never fails, laughs, laughs. It was when you went to work, right, on Russafa Street, yes, looks at, from there to Ruzafa Street, ugh, the shameless guy would come out, laughs, what else, what else, laughs, I went through a lot, I don't remember, like your friend when you went down there to the street, eh, nods, to the corner, and the train would pass, the train would pass, nods, and the cars would honk at you. Here's the translation of the provided Spanish text to English:

"Did they ever scare you, do you remember or not? Yes, back in those days, my goodness, and it wasn't that long ago, not that long ago. And Paquita, how long has it been since we last saw Paquita? Quite a while, she's not doing well. Not very well, is she better or worse than you? I don't know, she's not well. Yesterday on the avenue, yes, and you can count on your fingers the times I've run into her, of course. And her sister, of course, she's not doing well. The one who's doing very well is Paz. Oh, but now I heard she was in delicate health. Oh yes, the other day, I don't know who it was, said she's not well, yes, she was very unwell. Oh yes, shakes her head no. And so now, who, if she went to pick up the grandchildren and everything, haven't you seen her going to pick up the grandchildren? I haven't seen her, and I never hear anyone talk about her around here. No, last week we talked with her. I nod. With Paz, yes, you're getting confused, dear. Well, because time passes very quickly for you here. Uhm, the days pass very quickly for you. I don't remember, girl, it's okay, it's okay, I don't remember. Have you gone for a walk? Now a few days will pass, and they'll tell me this guy has gone home, laughs. And I'll say, oh, now I don't know, now I don't remember, laughs. Do you realize those things? And I'll say, it must be true, but it's okay because he'll greet you, of course. Yes, he'll greet you, you don't need to remember anything. I say that the women should tell me, that they should tell you, listen, that guy, and I don't remember. This happens to me a few times, and that's what scares you, that they tell you. Now it wouldn't happen to me because I wouldn't be scared now. Oh no, huh, damn, laughs. That was when you were young, but I don't go out calmly anymore. Oh, okay, but you never go alone anymore, of course. So what's going to happen to you, dear, don't make things up. You know how many times they used to scare me, this is getting repetitive, laughs. You see there on the bike, on the Vespa, that I'm not there, you're not on any Vespa, in that chair, that's dad. If it's not you, that's not me. Oh girl, you just told José Luis that this guy, that man, is dad. Oh, it's you, it's you, that's dad all along, down there in the rocking chair, you've got it mixed up. Well, and what about that?" translated: In a place in La Mancha, the name of which I do not wish to remember, not long ago lived a nobleman, one of those with a lance in the rack, an old shield, a lean horse, and a greyhound for racing. And that's what I want to see. But don't you have siblings or something over there? No, they're all here. I laugh, and another day, another time when we used to go to the beach. Oh yes, in Rubí, does it have a beach? Yes, there it does. And so we would go a little before lunch, and we would go into the water, and that's it. Very nice. You had a great time. Yes, yes, yes. translated: A place in La Mancha, whose name I do not wish to remember, not long ago lived a nobleman, one of those with a lance in the rack, an old shield, a skinny horse, and a fast greyhound. translated:
Tell him you have two grandchildren, talk to him about, well, what are their names, what are your grandchildren's names? I have two children, Juan and Mireia. Juan and Mireia, my son-in-law Juan too, Juanjo, and my daughter Paula and my son Roberto. This one was born on October 30, 1960, very well, and she on September 28, I don't know, 1963. *laughs* Where was I born? Where was I born? You were born in Rostecher, England. Uh, we went to England, my wife and I were there for five years, and she was born there, and another little girl who was lost, it was a cesarean, she was lost, and then this one came in Valencia, this one is Valencian. I set up a guesthouse and did everything next to the Astoria, the Astoria Hotel in Valencia, yes, on Vilaragut Street, there I set up a guesthouse, we named it Pensión Villa, like the river, ah, like a nation, Orinoco. *laughs* If it were a female, according to them, I would say when they gave birth, mister, this one is good for when she grows up to be a dairy cow, to take her away because I already understood about cows, but I was very congested, and three or four cows in heat came out, and my neighbor who took care of the cows and milked them like another, Mister Bolton and Sam didn't know, and they called a practitioner of those, and a veterinarian came, I'm very sick, mister, he says well covered, come on, I'll get you in the car, you know, I say all of them, mister, and the ones you have tied up too, he said like this, what are you telling me, I say all of them, tell me whichever you want, no, no, now I'm going to see, now let's go there, grab that cow, that one, three hundred twenty-three, look, yes sir, one hundred three hundred twenty-three, the other one, and he was handling them, the six or seven, eh, in heat, they are called in heat, they want a bull, uh-huh, they are already covered, I say all of them that are here, I tell you whichever you want, let's try with two, that one that goes by, grab it, what number does it have, Gómez, four hundred two, and the other six hundred twenty-three, for example, no, four hundred six hundred twenty-three, and the ones you have tied up, tell me whatever you want, I'm not taking you anymore, for what? *laughs* I believe it, he grabbed them by the band, I say, oh, this one fires one who is here. Here's the translation of the provided Spanish text into English:

"Twenty-four hours and you all have been here for twenty-four years and know nothing, and I had to go to their houses, eh, to my neighbor. I said, 'Hey Sam, it's not my fault. I didn't come to take your jobs away, eh, it's not my fault that you don't know how to do it.' I've been paying for fifty years, and now I have to pay another fifty years, what they were asking for, me and my bosses, yes, yes, now again Rajoy, Mr. Rajoy. Well, we don't know how it's going to work, but until August or September, we won't know how the pharmacy thing is going to work. You have to go with your income statement. I want a Gelocatil and show my income statement. Also, I don't know how many inspectors they're going to assign to those who have workers coming to their homes and such. If they're not insured, they'll have to assign thousands because some work a few hours. It's not like that. Oh, it's not those who were executed because they were Republicans, because in that plan, that's how Franco did it. He killed more after the war than during the war. Uh-huh, he has the most promiscuous granddaughters in Spain and the most drugged and drunk grandsons in Spain, that dictator, Franco's grandchildren, yes, yes, on television, drugged. When she married that guy, there were two or three guys there with them in bed, but promiscuous. Who? The one who married that guy from the north, yes, that on television, Franco's granddaughter, Carmen Collares, that was the woman, not Carmen Collares."

translated: In a place in La Mancha, the name of which I do not care to remember, not long ago lived a nobleman with a lance in the rack, a lean hack, and a greyhound for coursing. A pot of something more beef than mutton, with most nights having duels and scraps, lentils on Saturdays, and a pigeon added on Sundays, consumed three-quarters of his estate.

Sure, here's the translation of the text:

"But here I suppose they don't do house chores, right? You say they don't? Yes, of course, they send you. Do you bring your shirts? Of course, and do you mend them here? No, no, no, that won't be it. They might do something else, like, I don't know, like the mural over there of spring. Have you seen the mural over there, the poster? Maybe you did that too? I don't know, I don't remember. I've done so much that I don't remember anymore. Well, that's normal. *laughs* Yes, I do everything for him, whatever it is. I don't mind sewing for him. Uh-huh. And is he your only child? No, there are more, but they're in other places. *looks at trn* But tell the boy, the boy asks you. Look at him, he's another one. The one I live with is this one. Uh-huh. But if you have more children, he asks you. I don't know, José, Toni, how many do you have, my son? Right now, I'm not going to start counting if they're all over the place, each one on their own. But more or less, can you tell the boy? The boy asks you, Toni, Isa, how many are we? Do you remember more or less? Paco, I don't remember anymore. I've been like this for so long that I don't remember anymore. *laughs* trn You're dizzy from doing so much work. *laughs* trn *laughs* trn *laughs* *laughs* trn I've helped a lot, but now I'm fed up. Uh-huh, because it gets very tiring. Yes, you know how many stitches I've made in my life, and we keep going as long as we keep counting. I believe it, right? That's it. Do you remember more or less how many grandchildren you have? Not right now, I don't know. If I start counting them, maybe I can figure it out. Go ahead, start counting them, you count them. *laughs* trn *laughs* *laughs* trn The gaps, the gaps, you fill them right away, and the same question, and you're left without an answer. It makes a mess there or whatever."

translated: In a place in La Mancha, whose name I do not wish to remember, not long ago lived a nobleman, one of those with a lance and an old shield, a skinny horse, and a greyhound for racing. A pot of something more beef than mutton, hash most nights, duels and heartbreaks on Saturdays, lentils on Fridays, and some pigeon as an addition on Sundays consumed three-quarters of his estate.

translated: In a place in La Mancha, whose name I do not wish to remember, not long ago lived a nobleman, one of those with a lance and an ancient shield, a skinny horse, and a greyhound for racing. A pot of something more beef than mutton, a salad on most nights, scraps on Saturdays, lentils on Fridays, and a pigeon as an extra on Sundays consumed three-quarters of his income.

translated: In a place in La Mancha, whose name I do not wish to remember, not long ago lived a nobleman, one of those with a lance in the rack, an old shield, a skinny horse, and a fast greyhound. He had a pot of something more beef than mutton, hash most nights, scrambled eggs on Saturdays, lentils on Fridays, and a pigeon as an extra on Sundays, consuming three-quarters of his estate.

And in Serra, well, yes, many times what mothers want, yes, a little bit, or if not, or how many, because you have to tell mothers something, and many times mothers are left behind, and that can't be because mothers are very good, very good for you, for this one, for everyone, for everyone, for this beautiful one, look how pretty she is, how pretty. Saint Joseph, it's Saint Joseph, mine.
